items.title: efface studio site
items.summary: The official site for efface — our flagship, built by us.
items.description:
Our own site, built to demonstrate the exact capability efface offers as a studio.
Korean/English bilingual, a real-time quote estimator, an inquiry form gated by two-step email + SMS verification, security headers and a WAF — built and operated at production-service quality.
Below is a case study of the real troubleshooting we hit while building it, and the key features we implemented.
items.headline: Erase the complexity. Keep the effect.

troubleshooting.title: Infinite marquee loop jumped once per cycle
troubleshooting.problem: The tech-stack logo strip scrolls horizontally forever, but it visibly stuttered at the end of every cycle.
troubleshooting.cause: Rewinding the track with translateX(-50%) assumes the duplicate is exactly as wide as the original. But a container gap means N duplicated items produce 2N−1 gaps, not 2N — so the -50% rewind point was off by exactly one gap.
troubleshooting.solution: Removed the container gap and wrapped each chip in an equal right-padding wrapper, folding the spacing into the chip width. The duplicate then matches the original exactly.
troubleshooting.result: A perfectly seamless infinite loop with no visible seam.
troubleshooting.prLabel: #31 · components/sections/Marquee.tsx — removed container gap, added per-chip pr-4 shrink-0 wrapper

troubleshooting.title: Entry animation made loading feel slower
troubleshooting.problem: Hero content faded in on arrival, but users perceived the page as 'loading slowly'.
troubleshooting.cause: A JS motion library's entry animation only starts after hydration. So HTML already delivered by SSR sat invisible for a moment, looking like a blank screen.
troubleshooting.solution: Replaced the entry motion with CSS @keyframes. CSS animation plays on first paint without waiting for hydration; prefers-reduced-motion users are handled too.
troubleshooting.result: Content shows the instant it arrives, improving perceived load speed.
troubleshooting.prLabel: #37 dropped entry fades → #38 · app/globals.css + Hero.tsx — introduced CSS rise-in @keyframes

troubleshooting.title: OTP send blocked the API response
troubleshooting.problem: The inquiry-form email-code send API waited synchronously for the external mail send (hundreds of ms), making the response slow.
troubleshooting.cause: Mail delivery and the API response were on the same path, so the user waited out the full mail-server round trip.
troubleshooting.solution: Split the mail send off with Next.js's after() callback. Once the code row commits to the DB the response returns immediately; the mail goes out in the background.
troubleshooting.result: p50 response dropped below 500ms; send failures are tracked via separate logging.
troubleshooting.prLabel: #20 · app/api/apply/send-code/route.ts — moved the Resend send into an after() callback

features.title: Korean / English bilingual
features.detail: Built on next-intl with [locale] dynamic routing. Not just on-screen content — metadata and outbound email templates branch by locale too.

features.title: Two-step email + SMS OTP
features.detail: Blocks inquiry-form spam. HMAC-SHA256 code hashing and signed tokens implemented without an external library; SMS uses a SOLAPI integration with per-number and per-IP send limits.

features.title: Real-time quote estimator
features.detail: Pick a service type, page count, and add-on features — price and timeline compute live, and 'Apply with this spec' prefills the inquiry form with your selections.

features.title: Security fundamentals
features.detail: Full set of CSP / HSTS / X-Frame-Options headers, a Cloudflare WAF rate limit, form CSRF origin checks, a honeypot, and Zod input validation.

items.title: Internal ops admin
items.summary: An admin web app that runs the whole ops dashboard from one screen.
items.description:
Users, schedules, work logs, meeting notes, approvals, expenses — all the data ops needs in one place.
Includes a preview mode you can browse without login, plus role-based access control, fast search, and snappy routing.
items.headline: One screen for the whole operation.

items.title: Team branding site
items.summary: Colorful single-page site that captures the team's identity.
items.description:
A single-page site introducing the team and their activities.
Member colors are visualized as gradient badges, conveying the team's diversity. Deployed as a fast static site on Vercel.
items.headline: Built around the team's identity.

items.title: Korean-style e-commerce demo
items.summary: A Korean-style e-commerce demo with a Coupang/Musinsa feel.
items.description:
An e-commerce demo built around patterns familiar to Korean shoppers.
It ships with category/best/new/deal routing, product detail, a cart drawer, and search — all the flow of a production-ready store.
items.headline: From discovery to checkout, in one flow.

items.title: Fine-dining restaurant site
items.summary: A classic restaurant site in the spirit of Eleven Madison Park.
items.description:
Large serif type and restrained photography set the mood for a fine-dining site.
Includes menu, reservations, private events, gift cards, and a journal — everything an operating restaurant needs.
items.headline: A reservation, set in serif.

items.title: General hospital portal
items.summary: A general-hospital portal modeled after Asan Medical Center.
items.description:
A demo motivated by the information density and trust-building tone of Korean general hospital portals.
Ships with 6 main subpages (about · departments · doctors · checkup · reservation · support) and a 4-step booking form ready to run.
items.headline: Care, organised at a glance.

items.title: Mobile wedding invitation
items.summary: A mobile wedding invitation in the Mocheong/KakaoTalk style.
items.description:
A mobile wedding invitation that fits in a single scroll.
Full-bleed hero, D-day countdown, lightbox gallery, account accordion, location, and guestbook — every flow you'd actually use.
items.headline: Their day, in everyone's pocket.

Process.heading:
From inquiry to delivery —
in 4 clean steps.
Process.subheading: 2–4 weeks on average. We tell you exactly what you'll receive at each step.

steps.title: Inquiry
steps.desc: Send your project details. We reply within 1 business day.

steps.title: Scope & contract
steps.desc: Once scope, features, and timeline are confirmed — contract sent, 50% upfront.

steps.title: Design & build
steps.desc: Figma review, then build. Weekly staging previews so you see progress.

steps.title: Launch & handover
steps.desc: Domain, deployment, and ops guide. Final 50% on delivery.

FAQ.heading: Frequently asked questions.
FAQ.subheading: Not finding what you need? Add it to the application form — we'll cover the rest in our intro call.

items.q: How long does a project take?
items.a: Landing pages 1–2 weeks, company sites 2–3 weeks, e-commerce/web apps 3+ weeks depending on scope. You'll get an exact timeline with the first reply.
Based on page count, features, and design scope. You'll get an initial quote within 1 business day of submitting the form, then a final quote and contract after we align on scope.

items.q: How many design review rounds are included?
items.a: Standard includes 2 review rounds. Additional rounds are billed per round. Reasonable revisions within scope are no charge.

items.q: Who owns the code and rights?
items.a: Full source code and copyright transfer to the client after final payment. GitHub repo ownership is transferred along with it.

items.q: Do you handle domain and hosting?
items.a: Yes — domain purchase, plus Vercel/Cloudflare/AWS setup. We recommend keeping these accounts in your name so you control the recurring costs.
1 month of free bug fixes after delivery. After that, monthly retainers or per-issue support are both available.
50% upfront, 50% on delivery. Tax invoices available. Projects over USD 2,000 can be split into 3 payments by agreement.

items.q: Can you redesign an existing site?
items.a: Yes. We review the existing code first and decide together whether migration or a fresh build is the right path.
